nameMeanings.name: Terry
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Irish: from the Norman personal name T(h)erry (Old French Thierri), a short form of Theodoric, which is composed of the ancient Germanic elements theud ‘people, race’ + rīc ‘power(ful), rich’. Theodoric was the name of the Ostrogothic leader (c. 454–526) who invaded Italy in 488 and established his capital at Ravenna in 493. His name was sometimes taken as a derivative of Greek Theodōros (see Theodore ). An Anglo-Norman family of this name has been in County Cork, Ireland, since the 13th century.
Irish: sometimes an Anglicized (‘translated’) form of Gaelic Mac Toirdhealbhaigh (see Turley ).

variants.name: Terris
meanings: Scottish (Fife): habitational name from Tarras in Rafford (Morayshire).
Catalan and southern French (Pyrénées-Orientales): from a personal name derived from the ancient Germanic name Theodoric (see Terry ).
French (southern); Catalan (Terrís): topographic name derived from French terre, Catalan terra ‘ground, earth, land’.

variants.name: Tarry
meanings: English (Northamptonshire and Leicestershire; of Norman origin): variant of   Terry   1.

variants.name: Tyrie
meanings: Scottish (Angus and Glasgow): habitational name from Tyrie (Perthshire) or Tyrie (Aberdeenshire). The placenames are from Gaelic tìr ‘(arable) land’ + a placename forming suffix (perhaps) –in.
English (of Norman origin): variant of   Terry  .

variants.name: Theodore
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French/Haitian Serge, Henri, Pierre, Alide, Andre, Antoine, Benoit, Berthony, Chantal, Daphnee, Edouard, Guilene.

French and Mauritian (Théodore); West Indian (in Haiti, Martinique, and Guadeloupe Théodore); English and Welsh: from a personal name derived from Greek Theodōros, a compound of theos ‘God’ + dōron ‘gift’, which was relatively popular in the Middle Ages because of its auspicious meaning. The name was borne by several early Christian saints. There was considerable confusion with the ancient Germanic name Theodoric (see Terry ). In Wales, the personal name Theodore was also adopted as an approximation to the Welsh name Tewdwr (see Tudor ). In North America, this surname has absorbed various other European cognates and their derivatives, e.g. Greek Theodorakis , Macedonian Todorovski, and Serbian Todorović (see Todorovic ).

variants.name: Thierry
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Leonce, Olivier, Alain, Armand, Fernand.

French: from the Old French personal name Thierri, a short form of ancient Germanic Theodoric (see Terry ), composed of the elements theud ‘people, race’ + rīc ‘power(ful), rich’. This surname is also found in Germany.

History: Francis-Jean Thierry from France married Frances Bowen Blake in Marietta, OH, in 1815. Joseph Thierry from Germany married Susanna Plabuet probably in Detroit, MI, in 1846. Louis Thierry from Mulhouse in Haut-Rhin married Amelia Appelt probably in Michigan c. 1863.

variants.name: Thilges
meanings: German: patronymic from Tilge, a pet form of the ancient Germanic personal name Theodoric (see Terry ).

variants.name: Tear
meanings: English: nickname from Middle English ter(e) ‘of fine appearance or character’. The name may sometimes have been confused with Terry .
Possibly an altered form of German   Thier  .
Manx: variant of   Teare  .

variants.name: Tjarks
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Fritz, Hermann.

East Frisian and North German: patronymic from Tjark, a short form of the ancient Germanic personal name Theodoric (see Terry ), composed of the elements theud ‘people, race’ + rīc ‘power(ful), rich’.

nameMeanings.name: Edward
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

variants.name: Edoardo
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Eduardo
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Eideard
meanings: Gaelic equivalent of Edward .

variants.name: Édouard
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Edvard
meanings: form of English Edward .

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.
